cross_connections.description: Grandson and successor editor of the Springfield Republican — paper passed from grandfather to grandson through Sally Bowles + Thomas Hooker marriage.

cross_connections.display_label: Earlier abolitionist editor of The Emancipator from same Hooker tree

narrative_blocks.body: He was named for his paternal grandfather Rev. Richard Hooker, the Macon Georgia Presbyterian missionary who died at forty-nine in 1857. He graduated Yale in 1899 the same year his elder sister Aurelia died, and lived to age 89 in 1967.

narrative_blocks.header: Took over Springfield Republican from cousin Sam V
narrative_blocks.body: His maternal grandfather Samuel Bowles III had built the Springfield Republican into one of America's most influential newspapers from 1844 to 1878. When the fifth Samuel Bowles broke the three-generation editor pattern by not running the paper, Richard — his cousin and the son of Sally Bowles Hooker — took over as editor and general manager, carrying the newspaper from the Bowles family into the Hooker family.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Augusta Georgia, reared in New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: His Macon-born father Thomas Hooker was already settling toward a New Haven banking career when Richard was born at Augusta Georgia in 1878. The family soon settled at New Haven where Richard was reared and attended Yale, graduating with the class of 1899.

narrative_blocks.header: Washington correspondent before taking the Republican
narrative_blocks.body: After his year of European study he joined the Republican and served as its Washington correspondent from 1904 to 1911 — covering Roosevelt's late presidency and Taft's full term from the press galleries. He succeeded his grandfather Samuel Bowles III as publisher and editor in 1915.

narrative_blocks.header: Friend of Wilson, declined two presidential offers
narrative_blocks.body: He was a friend of President Woodrow Wilson and strongly supported him editorially during World War I and in the fight for the League of Nations. He declined Wilson's offer of Assistant Secretary of the Navy and an earlier offer from President Taft to serve as press secretary.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote Macmillan history of the Republican, 1924
narrative_blocks.body: He authored 'The Story of an Independent Newspaper' (Macmillan 1924), a history of his family's paper across nearly a century. He also wrote corporate histories of the Massachusetts Mutual and Aetna life insurance companies, drawing on his banking-family background.

He served as a director of the Associated Press from 1927 to 1934, near the peak of his Republican editorial career. After stepping down from the Republican Company presidency in 1932 he contributed editorials almost until his death at eighty-nine in his Longmeadow MA home in 1967.

education.notes: Year of study in Europe after Yale graduation.

career.notes: Succeeded his maternal grandfather Samuel Bowles III as publisher and editor 1915. (Per 1934 Time magazine jingle, the 5th Samuel Bowles broke the 3-generation editor pattern — Richard, the Bowles cousin via the Hooker marriage, took over.)
President of the Republican Company until 1932; contributed editorials almost until his 1967 death.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of a paleontologist who named a volcano
narrative_blocks.body: Her grandfather John Strong Newberry was a leading 19th-century American paleontologist and paleobotanist on the Columbia University School of Mines faculty for 24 years; he helped found the Geological Society of America. Two species — Eucyclogobius newberryi (goby) and Salmo newberryi (rainbow trout) — and Lake Newberry NY and Newberry Volcano in Oregon all bear his name.

cross_connections.display_label: his father-in-law, the famed Springfield publisher

bio.notes: Named "Thomas" — honoring the founding ancestor Thomas Hooker (H00001)

narrative_blocks.header: Carried Thomas Hooker's blood through both parents
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Macon Georgia in September 1849 to Rev. Richard Hooker and Aurelia Dwight, he was the third documented child of a Hooker-Hooker cousin marriage in the tree — and the first whose dual descent propagated biologically. Through his father he descended directly from Thomas Hooker via the John Hooker line of Northampton; through his mother via Mary Hooker Pierpont's 1690s marriage and the Edwards-Dwight succession to Pope of Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Macon-born, married a Springfield Republican daughter
narrative_blocks.body: He was born at Macon Georgia in September 1849, the only child of a Presbyterian minister and a Dwight bride. In 1874 he married Sarah Augusta 'Sally' Bowles of Springfield Massachusetts — daughter of Samuel Bowles III, the editor of the Springfield Republican newspaper, then one of America's most influential dailies.

narrative_blocks.header: Both sons graduated Yale, younger took Harvard Law
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Aurelia died at twenty-four in 1899; his elder son Richard graduated Yale in 1899 the same year of her death; his younger son Thomas Jr graduated Yale in 1903 and went on to Harvard Law in 1906. He himself attended Yale's Sheffield Scientific School for two years without completing the degree.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty-five years in New Haven banking and trusts
narrative_blocks.body: Returning to New Haven in 1877 after five years of study and travel abroad, he entered business and financial life. He became vice president of the New Haven Trust Company in 1895, its president from 1902 to 1909, and at the merger that created Union & New Haven Trust Company he stepped to vice president there — while simultaneously rising at the First National Bank to vice president 1902, president 1909.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with mother Aurelia, wife Sally, and Pope Dwight forebears

education.notes: Hopkins Grammar School in New Haven for early education — same school where his Dwight great-great-grandfather Pope Dwight had taught after his 1769 Yale graduation.
Yale MA 1872 — three years after BA.
Five years 1872-1877 of study and travel abroad and in this country; returned to New Haven 1877 to enter business and financial career.

career.notes: Vice president of New Haven Trust Company 1895-1902.
President of New Haven Trust Company 1902-1909, until its merger into Union & New Haven Trust Company.
President of First National Bank from 1909 — held position at time of Carrington 1918 biography.
VP of Union & New Haven Trust Company from 1909 (after merger of NH Trust Co).
Ten years on New Haven Board of Education, 1894-1904.

parents.father_research_notes: Now structurally wired to X02841 Samuel Bowles III Easter Egg.
parents.mother_research_notes: Now structurally wired to X02842 Mary Bowles Easter Egg.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT, with husband Thomas Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of the most famous American editor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Samuel Bowles III edited the Springfield Republican newspaper into one of the most influential American dailies of the Civil War and Reconstruction eras. She grew up in the publisher's household and married Thomas Hooker at Springfield on 30 June 1874 at age twenty-four.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost daughter Aurelia young, raised two Yale sons
narrative_blocks.body: She bore three children in New Haven between 1875 and 1882. Her daughter Aurelia died at twenty-four in 1899 — the same year her elder son Richard graduated Yale; her younger son Thomas Jr followed at Yale in 1903 and took Harvard Law in 1906.

education.notes: Two-year special course, Sheffield Scientific School
